revision.text:
== 大西覚養 == 大西覚養(おおにし かくよう)生没年不詳 は[[戦国時代]]の[[阿波国]]の小[[大名]]
[[大西家]]は[[鎌倉時代]]に[[荘官]]として[[京都]]より派遣されていた[[近藤家]]が[[土着]]･[[改姓]]したもの。
[[承久の乱]]で功のあった[[小笠原長清]]の子[[小笠原長経]]が、[[守護代]]として阿波池田に赴任してきた際[[小笠原家]]に属した。 [[南北朝時代]]には大西家と小笠原家は[[南朝]]方として戦っていたが、南朝方が不利になると小笠原家は三好家と[[和議]]を結びこの地を離れる。 ここに大西家は小笠原家より独立、戦国時代には阿波西部最大勢力となっていた。
阿波国における三好家の勢力が後退し、[[土佐]]の[[長宗我部家]]が台頭すると、四国の中央に位置する大西家の[[白地城]]は四国統一を目指す長宗我部元親の攻勢にあう。 攻撃を受けた白地城主であった大西覚養は、弟の[[大西頼信]]を人質として和議を結ぶ。 しかし、三好家が[[織田家]]に援軍を頼み長宗我部家に対し対決姿勢を鮮明にすると、覚養は和議の条件を破り戦闘準備に取り掛かる。 これを知った元親は[[天正]]5年([[1577年]])白地城の支城･[[田尾城]]をわずか2日で攻め落とすと、覚養は[[讃岐国]][[麻城]]へ逃げ延びた。 しかし、翌天正6年([[1578年]])には麻城も落城している。

== 大西覚養 == 大西覚養(おおにし かくよう)生没年不詳 は[[戦国時代]]の[[阿波国]]の小[[大名]]
[[大西家]]は[[鎌倉時代]]に[[荘官]]として[[京都]]より派遣されていた[[近藤家]]が[[土着]]･[[改姓]]したもの。
[[承久の乱]]で功のあった[[小笠原長清]]の子[[小笠原長経]]が、[[守護代]]として阿波池田に赴任してきた際[[小笠原家]]に属した。 [[南北朝時代]]には大西家と小笠原家は[[南朝]]方として戦っていたが、南朝方が不利になると小笠原家は[[細川家]]と[[和議]]を結びこの地を離れ三好と改姓する。 ここに大西家は小笠原家より独立、戦国時代には阿波西部最大勢力となっていた。
阿波国における[[三好家]]の勢力が後退し、[[土佐]]の[[長宗我部家]]が台頭すると、四国の中央に位置する大西家の[[白地城]]は四国統一を目指す長宗我部元親の攻勢にあう。 攻撃を受けた白地城主であった大西覚養は、弟の[[大西頼信]]を人質として和議を結ぶ。 しかし、三好家が[[織田家]]に援軍を頼み長宗我部家に対し対決姿勢を鮮明にすると、覚養は和議の条件を破り戦闘準備に取り掛かる。 これを知った元親は[[天正]]5年([[1577年]])白地城の支城･[[田尾城]]をわずか2日で攻め落とすと、覚養は[[讃岐国]][[麻城]]へ逃げ延びた。 しかし、翌天正6年([[1578年]])には麻城も落城している。

'''大西 覚養'''（おおにし かくよう）生没年不詳 は[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[阿波国]]の小[[大名]]
[[大西氏]]は[[鎌倉時代]]に[[荘官]]として[[京都]]より派遣されていた[[近藤氏]]が[[土着]]･[[改姓]]したもの。
[[承久の乱]]で功のあった[[小笠原長清]]の子[[小笠原長経]]が、[[守護代]]として阿波池田に赴任してきた際[[小笠原氏]]に属した。
[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]には大西氏と小笠原氏は[[南朝 (日本)|南朝]]方として戦っていたが、南朝方が不利になると小笠原氏は[[細川氏]]と[[和議]]を結びこの地を離れ三好と改姓する。 ここに大西氏は小笠原氏より独立、戦国時代には阿波西部最大勢力となっていた。
阿波国における[[三好氏]]の勢力が後退し、[[土佐国|土佐]]の[[長宗我部氏]]が台頭すると、四国の中央に位置する大西氏の[[白地城]]は四国統一を目指す長宗我部元親の攻勢にあう。 攻撃を受けた白地城主であった大西覚養は、弟の[[大西頼信]]を人質として和議を結ぶ。 しかし、三好氏が[[織田氏]]に援軍を頼み長宗我部氏に対し対決姿勢を鮮明にすると、覚養は和議の条件を破り戦闘準備に取り掛かる。 これを知った元親は[[天正]]5年([[1577年]])白地城の支城･[[田尾城]]をわずか2日で攻め落とすと、覚養は[[讃岐国]][[麻城]]へ逃げ延びた。 しかし、翌天正6年([[1578年]])には麻城も落城している。

'''大西 覚養'''（おおにし かくよう、生没年不詳）は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[阿波国]]の[[国人]][[領主]]。
[[大西氏]]は[[鎌倉時代]]に[[荘官]]として[[京都]]より派遣されていた[[近藤氏]]が[[土着]]･[[改姓]]したもの。
[[承久の乱]]で功のあった[[小笠原長清]]の子[[小笠原長経]]が、[[守護代]]として阿波池田に赴任してきた際[[小笠原氏]]に属した。
[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]には大西氏と小笠原氏は[[南朝 (日本)|南朝]]方として戦っていたが、南朝方が不利になると小笠原氏は[[細川氏]]と[[和議]]を結びこの地を離れ三好と改姓する。 ここに大西氏は小笠原氏より独立、戦国時代には阿波西部最大勢力となっていた。
阿波国における[[三好氏]]の勢力が後退し、[[土佐国|土佐]]の[[長宗我部氏]]が台頭すると、四国の中央に位置する大西氏の[[白地城]]は四国統一を目指す長宗我部元親の攻勢にあう。
攻撃を受けた白地城主であった大西覚養は、弟の[[大西頼信]]を人質として和議を結ぶ。
しかし、三好氏が[[織田氏]]に援軍を頼み長宗我部氏に対し対決姿勢を鮮明にすると、覚養は和議の条件を破り戦闘準備に取り掛かる。
これを知った元親は[[天正]]5年([[1577年]])白地城の支城･[[田尾城]]をわずか2日で攻め落とすと、覚養は[[讃岐国]][[麻城]]へ逃げ延びた。
しかし、翌天正6年([[1578年]])には麻城も落城している。

'''大西 覚養'''（おおにし かくよう、生没年不詳）は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[阿波国]]の[[国人]][[領主]]。
== 生涯 == [[大西氏]]は[[鎌倉時代]]に[[荘官]]として[[京都]]より派遣されていた[[近藤氏]]が[[土着]]･[[改姓]]したもの。
[[承久の乱]]で功のあった[[小笠原長清]]の子[[小笠原長経]]が、[[守護代]]として阿波池田に赴任してきた際[[小笠原氏]]に属した。
[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]には大西氏と小笠原氏は[[南朝 (日本)|南朝]]方として戦っていたが、南朝方が不利になると小笠原氏は[[細川氏]]と[[和議]]を結びこの地を離れ三好と改姓する。 ここに大西氏は小笠原氏より独立、戦国時代には阿波西部最大勢力となっていた。
大西覚養は[[三好長慶]]の妹を娶り、白地城を拠点に阿波・讃岐・伊予の辺境地帯を支配した。
阿波国における[[三好氏]]の勢力が後退し、[[土佐国|土佐]]の[[長宗我部氏]]が台頭すると、四国の中央に位置する大西氏の[[白地城]]は四国統一を目指す長宗我部元親の攻勢にあう。
攻撃を受けた白地城主であった大西覚養は、弟の[[大西頼信]]を人質として和議を結ぶ。
しかし、三好氏が[[織田氏]]に援軍を頼み長宗我部氏に対し対決姿勢を鮮明にすると、覚養は[[三好笑岩]]の求めに応じ、和議の条件を破り戦闘準備に取り掛かる。
これを知った元親は[[天正]]5年([[1577年]])白地城の支城･[[田尾城]]をわずか2日で攻め落とすと、覚養は[[讃岐国]][[麻城]]へ逃げ延びた。
しかし、翌天正6年([[1578年]])には麻城も落城している。
== 関連項目 == * [[阿波国]] * [[白地城]] * [[長宗我部元親]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:おおにし かくよう}} [[Category:戦国時代の人物 (日本)]] [[Category:阿波国の人物]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

阿波国における[[三好氏]]の勢力が後退し、[[土佐国|土佐]]の[[長宗我部氏]]が台頭すると、四国の中央に位置する大西氏の[[白地城]]は四国統一を目指す長宗我部元親の攻勢にあう。 攻撃を受けた白地城主の覚養は、弟の[[大西頼信]]を人質として和議を結ぶ。
しかし、三好氏が[[織田氏]]に援軍を頼み長宗我部氏に対し対決姿勢を鮮明にすると、覚養は[[三好笑岩]]の求めに応じ、和議の条件を破り戦闘準備に取り掛かる。 これを知った元親は、まずは三好氏の勢力を後退させ、続いて[[天正]]5年([[1577年]])白地城の支城･[[田尾城]]をわずか2日で攻め落とすと、覚養は[[讃岐国]][[麻城]]へ逃げ延びた。

大西覚養は[[三好長慶]]の妹を娶り、白地城を拠点に阿波・讃岐・伊予の辺境地帯を支配した。

阿波国における[[三好氏]]の勢力が後退し、[[土佐国|土佐]]の[[長宗我部氏]]が台頭すると、四国の中央に位置する大西氏の[[白地城]]は四国統一を目指す長宗我部元親の攻勢にあう。 城主の覚養は、弟の[[大西頼信]]を人質として一旦は和議を結んだ。
しかし、三好氏が[[織田氏]]に援軍を頼み長宗我部氏に対し対決姿勢を鮮明にすると、覚養は[[三好笑岩]]の求めに応じ、和議の条件を破り戦闘準備に取り掛かった。 これを知った元親は、まずは阿波の三好軍を破り、続いて[[天正]]5年([[1577年]])白地城の支城･[[田尾城]]をわずか2日で攻め落とすと、覚養は[[讃岐国]][[麻城]]へ逃げ延びた。
しかし、翌天正6年([[1578年]])には麻城も落城した。
元親に降伏した覚養は阿波に戻り、三好方に服属している[[重清城]]城主で娘婿の[[重清長政]]を頼り、長宗我部への降伏を勧めるも拒否されたのでこれを謀殺した。 元親は覚養に重清城の守備を任せるが、程なくして三好方の[[十河存保]]の反撃を受けて敗死した。

しかし、三好氏が[[織田氏]]に援軍を頼み長宗我部氏に対し対決姿勢を鮮明にすると、覚養は[[三好笑岩]]の求めに応じ、和議の条件を破り戦闘準備に取り掛かった。 これを知った元親は、まずは阿波の三好軍を破り、続いて[[天正]]5年([[1577年]])白地城の支城･[[田尾城]]をわずか2日で攻め落とすと、覚養は[[讃岐国]][[麻城]]へ逃げ延びた。
しかし、翌天正6年([[1578年]])には麻城も落城した。
元親に降伏した覚養は阿波に戻り、三好方に服属している[[重清城]]城主で娘婿の[[重清長政]]を頼り、長宗我部への降伏を勧めるも拒否されたのでこれを謀殺した。 覚養は元親より重清城の守備を任せられるが、程なくして三好方の[[十河存保]]の反撃を受けて敗死した。

阿波国における[[三好氏]]の勢力が後退し、[[土佐国|土佐]]の[[長宗我部氏]]が台頭すると、四国の中央に位置する白地城は四国統一を目指す[[長宗我部元親]]の攻勢にあう。 城主の覚養は、弟の[[大西頼包]]を人質として一旦は和議を結んだ。
しかし、三好氏が[[織田氏]]に援軍を頼み長宗我部氏に対し対決姿勢を鮮明にすると、覚養は[[三好笑岩]]の求めに応じ、和議の条件を破り戦闘準備に取り掛かった。 これを知った元親は、まずは阿波の三好軍を破り、続いて[[天正]]5年([[1577年]])白地城の支城･[[田尾城]]をわずか2日で攻め落とすと、覚養は[[讃岐国]][[麻城]]へ逃げ延びた。
しかし、翌天正6年(1578年)には麻城も落城し、覚養は元親に厚遇されていた頼包の勧めに応じて降伏した。
阿波に戻った覚養は、三好方に服属している[[重清城]]城主で娘婿の[[重清長政]]を頼り、長宗我部への降伏を勧めるも拒否されたのでこれを謀殺した。 覚養は元親より重清城の守備を任せられるが、程なくして三好方の[[十河存保]]の反撃を受けて敗死した。
== 関連項目 == * [[阿波国]] * [[白地城]] * [[三好長慶]] * [[長宗我部元親]]

[[承久の乱]]で功のあった[[小笠原長清]]の子[[小笠原長経]]が、[[守護代]]として阿波池田に赴任してきた際[[小笠原氏]]に属した。

しかし、翌天正6年(1578年)には麻城も落城し、覚養は元親に厚遇されていた頼包の勧めに応じて降伏した。

'''大西 覚養'''（おおにし かくよう、? - [[天正]]6年（[[1578年]]））は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[阿波国]]の[[国人]][[領主]]。出雲守と称す。覚用、角養とする史料もある。俗名は輝武。父は[[大西頼武]]、弟に頼晴、[[大西頼包|頼包]]。
== 生涯 == [[大西氏]]は[[鎌倉時代]]に[[荘官]]として[[京都]]より派遣されていた[[近藤氏]]が[[土着]]・[[改姓]]したもの。
[[承久の乱]]で功のあった[[小笠原長清]]の子[[小笠原長経]]が、[[守護代]]として阿波池田に赴任してきた際[[小笠原氏]]に属した。
[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]には大西氏と小笠原氏は[[南朝 (日本)|南朝]]方として戦っていたが、南朝方が不利になると小笠原氏は[[細川氏]]と[[和議]]を結びこの地を離れ三好と改姓する。ここに大西氏は小笠原氏より独立、戦国時代には阿波西部の最大勢力となっていた。
大西覚養の父・頼武は[[三好長慶]]の妹を娶り、また覚養自身も三好家の女を娶っており、三好家と密接な関係を築きあげ、[[白地城]]を拠点に阿波・讃岐・伊予の辺境地帯を支配した。
阿波国における[[三好氏]]の勢力が後退し、[[土佐国|土佐]]の[[長宗我部氏]]が台頭すると、四国の中央に位置する白地城は四国統一を目指す[[長宗我部元親]]の攻勢にあう。城主の覚養は、弟の[[大西頼包]]を人質として一旦は和議を結んだ。
しかし、三好氏が[[織田氏]]に援軍を頼み長宗我部氏に対し対決姿勢を鮮明にすると、覚養は[[三好笑岩]]の求めに応じ、和議の条件を破り戦闘準備に取り掛かった。それを知った元親は、まずは阿波の三好軍を破り、続いて[[天正]]5年（[[1577年]]）白地城の支城・[[田尾城]]をわずか2日で攻め落とすと、覚養は[[讃岐国]][[麻城]]へ逃げ延びた。
しかし、翌天正6年（1578年）には麻城も落城し、覚養は元親に厚遇されていた頼包の勧めに応じて降伏した。
阿波に戻った覚養は、三好方に服属している[[重清城]]城主で娘婿の[[重清長政]]を頼り、長宗我部への降伏を勧めるも拒否されたのでこれを謀殺した。覚養は元親より重清城の守備を任せられるが、程なくして三好方の[[十河存保]]の反撃を受けて敗死した。
== 関連項目 == * [[阿波国]] * [[白地城]] * [[三好長慶]] * [[長宗我部元親]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:おおにし かくよう}} [[Category:戦国武将(日本)]] [[Category:阿波国の人物]] [[Category:1578年没]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{出典の明記|date=2019年11月}} '''大西 覚養'''（おおにし かくよう、? - [[天正]]6年（[[1578年]]））は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[阿波国]]の[[国人]][[領主]]。出雲守と称す。覚用、角養とする史料もある。俗名は輝武。父は[[大西頼武]]、弟に頼晴、[[大西頼包|頼包]]。
== 生涯 == [[大西氏]]は[[鎌倉時代]]に[[荘官]]として[[京都]]より派遣されていた[[近藤氏]]が[[土着]]・[[改姓]]したもの。[[承久の乱]]で功のあった[[小笠原長清]]の子[[小笠原長経]]が、[[守護代]]として阿波池田に赴任してきた際[[小笠原氏]]に属した。[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]には大西氏と小笠原氏は[[南朝 (日本)|南朝]]方として戦っていたが、南朝方が不利になると小笠原氏は[[細川氏]]と[[和議]]を結びこの地を離れ三好と改姓する。ここに大西氏は小笠原氏より独立、戦国時代には阿波西部の最大勢力となっていた。
大西覚養の父・頼武は[[三好長慶]]の妹を娶り、また覚養自身も三好家の女を娶っており、三好家と密接な関係を築きあげ、[[白地城]]を拠点に阿波・讃岐・伊予の辺境地帯を支配した。阿波国における[[三好氏]]の勢力が後退し、[[土佐国|土佐]]の[[長宗我部氏]]が台頭すると、四国の中央に位置する白地城は四国統一を目指す[[長宗我部元親]]の攻勢にあう。城主の覚養は、弟の[[大西頼包]]を人質として一旦は和議を結んだ。
しかし、三好氏が[[織田氏]]に援軍を頼み長宗我部氏に対し対決姿勢を鮮明にすると、覚養は[[三好康長|三好笑岩]]の求めに応じ、和議の条件を破り戦闘準備に取り掛かった。それを知った元親は、まずは阿波の三好軍を破り、続いて[[天正]]5年（[[1577年]]）白地城の支城・[[田尾城]]をわずか2日で攻め落とすと、覚養は[[讃岐国]][[麻城]]へ逃げ延びた。天正6年（1578年）には麻城も落城し、覚養は元親に厚遇されていた頼包の勧めに応じて降伏した。
阿波に戻った覚養は、三好方に服属している[[重清城]]城主で娘婿の[[重清長政]]を頼り、長宗我部への降伏を勧めるも拒否されたのでこれを謀殺した。覚養は元親より重清城の守備を任せられるが、程なくして三好方の[[十河存保]]の反撃を受けて敗死した。
== 関連項目 == * [[阿波国]] * [[白地城]] * [[三好長慶]] * [[長宗我部元親]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:おおにし かくよう}} [[Category:戦国武将]] [[Category:近藤氏]] [[Category:阿波国の人物]] [[Category:1578年没]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

大西覚養の父・頼武は[[三好長慶]]の妹を娶り、また覚養自身も三好実休の娘を娶っており、三好家と密接な関係を築きあげ、[[白地城]]を拠点に阿波・讃岐・伊予の辺境地帯を支配した。阿波国における[[三好氏]]の勢力が後退し、[[土佐国|土佐]]の[[長宗我部氏]]が台頭すると、四国の中央に位置する白地城は四国統一を目指す[[長宗我部元親]]の攻勢にあう。城主の覚養は、弟の[[大西頼包]]を人質として一旦は和議を結んだ。

{{出典の明記|date=2019年11月}} '''大西 覚養'''（おおにし かくよう、? - [[天正]]6年（[[1578年]]））は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[阿波国]]の[[国人]][[領主]]。出雲守と称す。覚用、角養とする史料もある。俗名は輝武。父は[[大西頼武]]、弟に頼晴、[[大西頼包|頼包]]。

大西覚養の父・頼武は[[三好長慶]]の妹を娶り、また覚養自身も伯父・三好実休の娘を娶っており、三好家と密接な関係を築きあげ、[[白地城]]を拠点に阿波・讃岐・伊予の辺境地帯を支配した。阿波国における[[三好氏]]の勢力が後退し、[[土佐国|土佐]]の[[長宗我部氏]]が台頭すると、四国の中央に位置する白地城は四国統一を目指す[[長宗我部元親]]の攻勢にあう。城主の覚養は、弟の[[大西頼包]]を人質として一旦は和議を結んだ。

He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武}},..."

{{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 6 [1578]) was a {{ill|daimyo|ja|大名}} and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人}} lord of {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}, who were appointed as governors during the rule of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|ja|小笠原長清}}. By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏}}. From his base at {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国}}, Shiraji Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Shiraji Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} * {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}} * {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}} * {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Sengoku period samurai]] [[Category:pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Kondō clan]] [[Category:pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:People of Awa Province (Tokushima)]] [[Category:pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:1578 deaths]]
[[ja:大西覚養]]

{{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 6 [1578]) was a {{ill|daimyo|ja|大名}} and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人}} lord of {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}, who were appointed as governors during the rule of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|ja|小笠原長清}}. By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏}}. From his base at {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国}}, Shiraji Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Shiraji Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} * {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}} * {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}} * {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}

By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏}}. From his base at {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国}}, Shiraji Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Shiraji Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.

By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.

When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.

Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏}}. From his base at {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国}}, Shiraji Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Shiraji Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.

{{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 6 [1578]) was a {{ill|daimyo|ja|大名}} and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人}} lord of {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}, who were appointed as governors during the rule of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|ja|小笠原長清}}. By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏}}. From his base at {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国}}, Shiraji Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Shiraji Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} * {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}} * {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}} * {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}

From his base at {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国}}, Shiraji Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Shiraji Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.

The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.

{{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 6 [1578]) was a {{ill|daimyo|ja|大名}} and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人}} lord of {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}, who were appointed as governors during the rule of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|ja|小笠原長清}}. By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏}}. From his base at {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国}}, Shiraji Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Shiraji Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} * {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}} * {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}} * {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}

{{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 6 [1578]) was a {{ill|daimyo|ja|大名}} and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人}} lord of {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}, who were appointed as governors during the rule of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|ja|小笠原長清}}. By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏}}. From his base at {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国}}, Shiraji Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Shiraji Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.

{{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 6 [1578]) was a {{ill|daimyo|ja|大名}} and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人}} lord of {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}, who were appointed as governors during the rule of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|ja|小笠原長清}}. By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏}}. From his base at {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国}}, Shiraji Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Shiraji Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.

{{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 6 [1578]) was a {{ill|daimyo|ja|大名}} and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人}} lord of {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}, who were appointed as governors during the rule of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|ja|小笠原長清}}. By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏}}. From his base at {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国}}, Shiraji Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Shiraji Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.

{{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 6 [1578]) was a {{ill|daimyo|ja|大名}} and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人}} lord of {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}, who were appointed as governors during the rule of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|ja|小笠原長清}}. By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏}}. From his base at {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国}}, Shiraji Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Shiraji Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} * {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}} * {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}} * {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}

{{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 6 [1578]) was a {{ill|daimyo|ja|大名}} and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人}} lord of {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}, who were appointed as governors during the rule of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|ja|小笠原長清}}. By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏}}. From his base at {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国}}, Shiraji Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Shiraji Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.

{{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 6 [1578]) was a {{ill|daimyo|ja|大名}} and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人}} lord of {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}, who were appointed as governors during the rule of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|ja|小笠原長清}}. By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏}}. From his base at {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国}}, Shiraji Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Shiraji Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} * {{ill|Shiraji Castle|ja|白地城}} * {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}} * {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}

{{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 6 [1578]) was a {{ill|daimyo|ja|大名}} and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人}} lord of {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}, who were appointed as governors during the rule of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|ja|小笠原長清}}. By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏}}. From his base at {{ill|Hakuchi Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.

In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Hakuchi Castle's satellite fortifications. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.

{{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era}} 6 [1578]) was a {{ill|daimyo|ja|大名}} and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人}} lord of {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}, who were appointed as governors during the rule of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|ja|小笠原長清}}. By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏}}. From his base at {{ill|Hakuchi Castle|ja|白地城}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国}}, Hakuchi Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Hakuchi Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保}}, a Miyoshi ally.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国}} * {{ill|Hakuchi Castle|ja|白地城}} * {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶}} * {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親}}

== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏|12=draft}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏|12=draft}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏|12=draft}}, who were appointed as governors during the rule of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|ja|小笠原長清|12=draft}}. By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|12=draft}}, the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏|12=draft}} and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶|12=draft}}, while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of {{ill|Miyoshi Jikkyū|ja|三好実休|12=draft}}, cementing strong ties with the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|ja|三好氏|12=draft}}. From his base at {{ill|Hakuchi Castle|ja|白地城|12=draft}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国|12=draft}}.
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the {{ill|Chōsokabe clan|ja|長宗我部氏|12=draft}} rose in {{ill|Tosa Province|ja|土佐国|12=draft}}, Hakuchi Castle became a key target for {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親|12=draft}}, who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏|12=draft}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Hakuchi Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城|12=draft}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城|12=draft}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城|12=draft}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保|12=draft}}, a Miyoshi ally.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hakuchi Castle|ja|白地城|12=draft}} * {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|ja|三好長慶|12=draft}} * {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親|12=draft}}

{{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft}} 6 [1578]) was a {{ill|daimyo|ja|大名|12=draft}} and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人|12=draft}} lord of {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|ja|阿波国|12=draft}} during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武|12=draft}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包|12=draft}}.

However, when the Miyoshi sought {{ill|Oda clan|ja|織田氏|12=draft}} support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Hakuchi Castle's satellite fortifications. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft}} 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城|12=draft}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城|12=draft}} in Sanuki. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by {{ill|Sogō Masayasu|ja|十河存保|12=draft}}, a Miyoshi ally.

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2019年11月}} '''大西 覚養'''（おおにし かくよう、? - [[天正]]6年（[[1578年]]））は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[阿波国]]の[[国人]][[領主]]。出雲守と称す。覚用、角養とする史料もある。俗名は輝武。父は[[大西頼武]]、弟に頼晴、[[大西頼包|頼包]]。
== 生涯 == [[大西氏]]は[[鎌倉時代]]に[[荘官]]として[[京都]]より派遣されていた[[近藤氏]]が[[土着]]・[[改姓]]したもの。[[承久の乱]]で功のあった[[小笠原長清]]の子[[小笠原長経]]が、[[守護代]]として阿波池田に赴任してきた際[[小笠原氏]]に属した。[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]には大西氏と小笠原氏は[[南朝 (日本)|南朝]]方として戦っていたが、南朝方が不利になると小笠原氏は[[細川氏]]と[[和議]]を結びこの地を離れ三好と改姓する。ここに大西氏は小笠原氏より独立、戦国時代には阿波西部の最大勢力となっていた。
大西覚養の父・頼武は[[三好長慶]]の妹を娶り、また覚養自身も伯父・三好実休の娘を娶っており、三好家と密接な関係を築きあげ、[[白地城]]を拠点に阿波・讃岐・伊予の辺境地帯を支配した。阿波国における[[三好氏]]の勢力が後退し、[[土佐国|土佐]]の[[長宗我部氏]]が台頭すると、四国の中央に位置する白地城は四国統一を目指す[[長宗我部元親]]の攻勢にあう。城主の覚養は、弟の[[大西頼包]]を人質として一旦は和議を結んだ。
しかし、三好氏が[[織田氏]]に援軍を頼み長宗我部氏に対し対決姿勢を鮮明にすると、覚養は[[三好康長|三好笑岩]]の求めに応じ、和議の条件を破り戦闘準備に取り掛かった。それを知った元親は、まずは阿波の三好軍を破り、続いて[[天正]]5年（[[1577年]]）白地城の支城・[[田尾城]]をわずか2日で攻め落とすと、覚養は[[讃岐国]][[麻城]]へ逃げ延びた。天正6年（1578年）には麻城も落城し、覚養は元親に厚遇されていた頼包の勧めに応じて降伏した。
阿波に戻った覚養は、三好方に服属している[[重清城]]城主で娘婿の[[重清長政]]を頼り、長宗我部への降伏を勧めるも拒否されたのでこれを謀殺した。覚養は元親より重清城の守備を任せられるが、程なくして三好方の[[十河存保]]の反撃を受けて敗死した。
== 関連項目 == * [[阿波国]] * [[白地城]] * [[三好長慶]] * [[長宗我部元親]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:おおにし かくよう}} [[Category:戦国武将]] [[Category:近藤氏]] [[Category:阿波国の人物]] [[Category:1578年没]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

阿波に戻った覚養は、三好方に服属している[[重清城]]城主で娘婿の[[重清長政]]を頼り、長宗我部への降伏を勧めるも拒否されたのでこれを謀殺した。覚養は元親より重清城の守備を任せられるが、程なくして三好方の[[十河存保]]の反撃を受けて敗死した。

Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Hakuchi Castle's satellite fortifications. In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tao Castle|qq=}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masanuki Castle|qq=}} in Sanuki. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. {{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 [1578]) was a [[:en:Daimyo|daimyo]] and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kokujin|qq=}} lord of [[:en:Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province (Tokushima)]] during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi Yorifusa|qq=}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi Yorikazu|qq=}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi clan|qq=}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kondō clan|qq=}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]], who were appointed as governors during the rule of [[:en:Ogasawara Nagakiyo|Ogasawara Nagakiyo]]. By the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of [[:en:Miyoshi Yoshikata|Miyoshi Jikkyū]], cementing strong ties with the [[:en:Miyoshi clan|Miyoshi clan]]. From his base at {{ill|Hakuchi Castle|ja|白地城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuchi Castle|qq=}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]], and [[:en:Iyo Province|Iyo Province]].
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the [[:en:Chōsokabe clan|Chōsokabe clan]] rose in [[:en:Tosa Province|Tosa Province]], Hakuchi Castle became a key target for [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]], who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Hakuchi Castle's satellite fortifications. In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tao Castle|qq=}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masanuki Castle|qq=}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigekiyo Castle|qq=}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by [[:en:Sogō Masayasu|Sogō Masayasu]], a Miyoshi ally.
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province (Tokushima)]] * {{ill|Hakuchi Castle|ja|白地城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuchi Castle|qq=}} * [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]] * [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

From his base at {{ill|Hakuchi Castle|ja|白地城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuchi Castle|qq=}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]], and [[:en:Iyo Province|Iyo Province]].
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the [[:en:Chōsokabe clan|Chōsokabe clan]] rose in [[:en:Tosa Province|Tosa Province]], Hakuchi Castle became a key target for [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]], who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Hakuchi Castle's satellite fortifications. In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tao Castle|qq=}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masanuki Castle|qq=}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigekiyo Castle|qq=}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by [[:en:Sogō Masayasu|Sogō Masayasu]], a Miyoshi ally.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438921]] Wikidata name: Ōnishi Kakuyō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 [1578]) was a [[:en:Daimyo|daimyo]] and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kokujin|qq=}} lord of [[:en:Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province (Tokushima)]] during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi Yorifusa|qq=}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi Yorikazu|qq=}}.

After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]], who were appointed as governors during the rule of [[:en:Ogasawara Nagakiyo|Ogasawara Nagakiyo]]. By the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. However, when the Miyoshi sought [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Hakuchi Castle's satellite fortifications. In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tao Castle|qq=}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masanuki Castle|qq=}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigekiyo Castle|qq=}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by [[:en:Sogō Masayasu|Sogō Masayasu]], a Miyoshi ally.

As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the [[:en:Chōsokabe clan|Chōsokabe clan]] rose in [[:en:Tosa Province|Tosa Province]], Hakuchi Castle became a key target for [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]], who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Hakuchi Castle's satellite fortifications. In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tao Castle|qq=|1=Tao Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576557}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masanuki Castle|qq=|1=Masanuki Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30922246}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigekiyo Castle|qq=|1=Shigekiyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109363543}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by [[:en:Sogō Masayasu|Sogō Masayasu]], a Miyoshi ally.

{{Infobox person|death_date=1578|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|military commander|qid=Q11545923}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438921]] Wikidata name: Ōnishi Kakuyō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 [1578]) was a [[:en:Daimyo|daimyo]] and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kokujin|qq=|1=kokujin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q859955}} lord of [[:en:Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province (Tokushima)]] during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi Yorifusa|qq=|1=Ōnishi Yoritake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438939}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi Yorikazu|qq=|1=Ōnishi Yorikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438937}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kondō clan|qq=|1=Kondō clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638843}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]], who were appointed as governors during the rule of [[:en:Ogasawara Nagakiyo|Ogasawara Nagakiyo]]. By the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of [[:en:Miyoshi Yoshikata|Miyoshi Jikkyū]], cementing strong ties with the [[:en:Miyoshi clan|Miyoshi clan]]. From his base at {{ill|Hakuchi Castle|ja|白地城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuchi Castle|qq=}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]], and [[:en:Iyo Province|Iyo Province]].
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the [[:en:Chōsokabe clan|Chōsokabe clan]] rose in [[:en:Tosa Province|Tosa Province]], Hakuchi Castle became a key target for [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]], who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Hakuchi Castle's satellite fortifications. In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tao Castle|qq=|1=Tao Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576557}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masanuki Castle|qq=|1=Masanuki Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30922246}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigekiyo Castle|qq=|1=Shigekiyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109363543}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by [[:en:Sogō Masayasu|Sogō Masayasu]], a Miyoshi ally.

== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kondō clan|qq=|1=Kondō clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638843}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]], who were appointed as governors during the rule of [[:en:Ogasawara Nagakiyo|Ogasawara Nagakiyo]]. By the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of [[:en:Miyoshi Yoshikata|Miyoshi Jikkyū]], cementing strong ties with the [[:en:Miyoshi clan|Miyoshi clan]]. From his base at {{ill|Hakuchi Castle|ja|白地城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuchi Castle|qq=}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]], and [[:en:Iyo Province|Iyo Province]].
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the [[:en:Chōsokabe clan|Chōsokabe clan]] rose in [[:en:Tosa Province|Tosa Province]], Hakuchi Castle became a key target for [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]], who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Hakuchi Castle's satellite fortifications. In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tao Castle|qq=|1=Tao Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576557}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masanuki Castle|qq=|1=Masanuki Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30922246}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigekiyo Castle|qq=|1=Shigekiyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109363543}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by [[:en:Sogō Masayasu|Sogō Masayasu]], a Miyoshi ally.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大西覚養]] {{Infobox person|death_date=1578|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|military commander|WD=Q11545923}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438921]] Wikidata name: Ōnishi Kakuyō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=November 2019}} '''Ōnishi Kakuyō''' (大西 覚養, ? - [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 [1578]) was a [[:en:Daimyo|daimyo]] and {{ill|kokujin|ja|国人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kokujin|1=kokujin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q859955}} lord of [[:en:Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province (Tokushima)]] during Japan's {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}. He held the title of Izumo-no-kami. Historical records also refer to him as Kakuyō or Kaku-yō, and his common name was Terutake. His father was {{ill|Ōnishi Yorifusa|ja|大西頼武|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi Yorifusa|1=Ōnishi Yoritake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438939}}, and he had younger brothers Yoriharu and {{ill|Ōnishi Yorikazu|ja|大西頼包|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi Yorikazu|1=Ōnishi Yorikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438937}}.
== Life == The {{ill|Ōnishi clan|ja|大西氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} originated from the {{ill|Kondō clan|ja|近藤氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kondō clan|1=Kondō clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638843}}, sent from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} as estate stewards (shōkan) during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. After settling in Awa and changing their family name, they served the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]], who were appointed as governors during the rule of [[:en:Ogasawara Nagakiyo|Ogasawara Nagakiyo]]. By the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the Ōnishi and Ogasawara clans fought for the Southern Court, but when the tide turned, the Ogasawara allied with the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and departed, leaving the Ōnishi clan independent. By the Sengoku period, the Ōnishi had become the dominant power in western Awa.
Ōnishi Kakuyō's father, Yorifusa, married a sister of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], while Kakuyō himself married a daughter of [[:en:Miyoshi Yoshikata|Miyoshi Jikkyū]], cementing strong ties with the [[:en:Miyoshi clan|Miyoshi clan]]. From his base at {{ill|Hakuchi Castle|ja|白地城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuchi Castle|qq=}}, Kakuyō controlled the borderlands of Awa, [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]], and [[:en:Iyo Province|Iyo Province]].
As the Miyoshi clan's influence in Awa waned and the [[:en:Chōsokabe clan|Chōsokabe clan]] rose in [[:en:Tosa Province|Tosa Province]], Hakuchi Castle became a key target for [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]], who sought to unify Shikoku. Initially, Kakuyō sent his younger brother, Yorikazu, as a hostage to Motochika, agreeing to a truce. However, when the Miyoshi sought [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] support and took a more aggressive stance, Kakuyō broke the truce and prepared for war.
In response, Motochika swiftly defeated the Miyoshi forces in Awa and attacked Hakuchi Castle's satellite fortifications. In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 5 (1577), he captured {{ill|Tao Castle|ja|田尾城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tao Castle|1=Tao Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576557}} in just two days, forcing Kakuyō to flee to {{ill|Masanuki Castle|ja|麻城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masanuki Castle|1=Masanuki Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30922246}} in Sanuki. The following year, Masanuki Castle fell, and Kakuyō surrendered on the advice of Yorikazu, who had been treated favorably by Motochika.
Returning to Awa, Kakuyō sought refuge with his son-in-law, Shigekiyo Nagamasa, the lord of {{ill|Shigekiyo Castle|ja|重清城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigekiyo Castle|1=Shigekiyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109363543}}. When Nagamasa refused Kakuyō's call to surrender to the Chōsokabe, Kakuyō betrayed him and orchestrated his assassination. Motochika then entrusted Shigekiyo Castle to Kakuyō, but shortly afterward, Kakuyō was defeated and killed in a counterattack by [[:en:Sogō Masayasu|Sogō Masayasu]], a Miyoshi ally.
